title: The Complete Project Management Body of Knowledge in One Video (PMBOK 7th Edition)

sections.title: The 12 Principles of Project Management
sections.intro: PMBOK 7th Edition opens by outlining twelve guiding principles that define how project managers should think and behave across all project contexts.

subsections.title: Stewardship
points: Be a diligent, respectful, and caring steward on behalf of the organization and stakeholders.
Stewardship implies a duty of care and accountability for project outcomes.

subsections.title: Team
points: Create a collaborative team environment where people can work effectively together.
Foster trust, shared ownership, and cooperation among project team members.

subsections.title: Stakeholders
points: Effectively engage with stakeholders throughout the project.
Understand stakeholder needs and ensure their involvement supports project success.

subsections.title: Value
points: Maintain a constant focus on the value being delivered to the business, organization, or customers.
All project decisions and activities should be evaluated against the value they contribute.

subsections.title: Systems Thinking
points: Recognize, evaluate, and respond to the many system interactions present in a project.
Projects operate within complex, interconnected environments that must be understood holistically.

subsections.title: Leadership
points: Demonstrate leadership behaviors that help team members and stakeholders thrive.
Leadership is about influencing and motivating people, not just managing tasks.

subsections.title: Tailoring
points: Tailor the project approach based on the organizational context, content, or product being delivered.
There is no one-size-fits-all method; adapt processes to fit the specific situation.

subsections.title: Quality
points: Build quality into processes and deliverables from the start to prevent mistakes.
Proactive quality management reduces rework and ensures outputs meet requirements.

subsections.title: Complexity
points: Navigate complexity by recognizing the dynamic, interconnected factors that affect projects.
Use appropriate tools and judgment to manage ambiguity and unpredictable interactions.

subsections.title: Risk
points: Optimize risk responses by continuously uncovering and addressing risks in the project.
Both threats and opportunities should be identified and actively managed.

subsections.title: Adaptability and Resiliency
points: Embrace adaptability and resiliency to respond effectively to changing conditions.
Help the team build the capacity to absorb disruption and adjust course as needed.

subsections.title: Change
points: Enable change to achieve the envisioned future state, recognizing that projects are fundamentally about change.
Project managers must champion change and prepare stakeholders to transition to new ways of working.

sections.title: Stakeholder Performance Domain
sections.intro: The Stakeholder Performance Domain focuses on building productive working relationships, securing stakeholder agreement with project objectives, and keeping beneficiaries supportive and satisfied throughout delivery.

subsections.title: Desired Outcomes
points: Productive working relationships with all stakeholders
Stakeholder agreement with and support for project objectives
Beneficiaries remain satisfied with what is being delivered

subsections.title: Key Terms and Stakeholder Definition
points: A stakeholder is anyone affected by the project or perceived to be affected by it
Circles of influence expand outward from the project manager to governing bodies, PMOs, steering committees, suppliers, customers, end users, and regulatory bodies

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement Steps
points: Identify stakeholders — using tools such as an organizational chart
Understand stakeholders — using a stakeholder engagement assessment matrix or similar tool
Analyze stakeholders — using a salience chart or an impact-vs-influence chart
Prioritize stakeholders to ensure the right people are engaged at the right times
Engage stakeholders regularly and monitor that engagement on an ongoing basis

subsections.title: Communication Methods
points: Engagement and communication channels include meetings, phone calls, brainstorming sessions, and product demos
Push communication: sender pushes information out via email or telephone calls
Pull communication: recipients retrieve information themselves from bulletin boards, websites, or SharePoint sites
Quick feedback loops verify whether the recipient understood the message, agrees with it, and has all the information needed

sections.title: Team Performance Domain
sections.intro: Team performance focuses on achieving shared ownership, high-performing teams, and leadership demonstrated at all levels of the project.

subsections.title: Outcomes and Key Terms
points: Desired outcomes: shared project ownership, high-performing teams, and leadership displayed by all team members.
Key roles: project manager, project management team (managing), and project team (doing the work).
Management = meeting objectives, maintaining effective processes, and monitoring work.
Leadership = growing people, influencing, motivating, listening, and enabling the team.

subsections.title: Centralized vs. Distributed Leadership
points: Centralized leadership: accountability rests on one person (e.g., the project manager); a project charter can clarify scope and roles.
Distributed leadership: shared across the team; teams are self-organizing with a facilitator, coach, or lead role.
Distributed teams have autonomy to operate as needed, as long as project goals are met.
Servant leadership: leaders remove blockers, shield the team from interruptions, and focus on team growth and development.

subsections.title: Team Development and Culture
points: Team development covers: clear vision and objectives, defined roles and responsibilities, team operating norms, and ongoing growth.
Project managers must establish a safe, respectful environment that enables open communication (psychological safety).
Cultural values include: transparency, integrity, respect, positive discourse, courage, and celebrating success.
Support behaviors: encouragement, empathy, and active listening.

subsections.title: High-Performing Team Factors
points: Open communication and shared understanding of work and goals.
Shared ownership, trust, and collaboration among team members.
Adaptability and resilience when facing change.
Empowerment to take necessary actions and recognition for accomplishments.

subsections.title: Leadership Skills
points: Establishing vision: developed collaboratively with stakeholders; answers what the project is for, what it delivers, and what defines success.
Critical thinking: awareness of personal bias, researching and analyzing data; uses deductive reasoning (general to specific) and inductive reasoning (specific to general).
Motivation: intrinsic (belief in work, achievement, self-direction, relatedness, personal growth) vs. extrinsic (money, bonuses, status).
Emotional intelligence: self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, and social skill.
Decision making and conflict management are also essential leadership skills.

subsections.title: Conflict Management
points: Keep communication respectful throughout conflicts.
Focus on the issue, not the person involved.
Address the present situation; avoid bringing up past grievances.
Search for alternatives and solutions collaboratively.

subsections.title: Tailoring Leadership Styles
points: Consider team members' experience with the type of project being undertaken.
Account for the maturity level of project team members.
Be aware of organizational governance structures that may constrain leadership approach.
For distributed teams, determine what communication tools are needed (video, chat, messaging) based on team location.

sections.title: Development Approach and Life Cycle Domain
sections.intro: This domain covers how the chosen development approach, delivery cadence, and life cycle phases must align with the nature of the deliverables and project context.

subsections.title: Domain Outcomes and Key Terms
points: The development approach must be consistent with the deliverables being created.
The project life cycle should connect value delivery to all stakeholders from start to finish.
Deliverable: the product or feature being produced.
Development approach: the method used to create the deliverable.
Cadence: the rhythm of meetings and interactions on the project.
Project phase: related activities that complete a deliverable; life cycle = all phases combined.

subsections.title: Delivery Cadence
points: The type of deliverable, risk/uncertainty, complexity, and need for change all influence cadence.
Single delivery: the entire product is delivered once.
Multiple deliveries: several features are delivered across the project.
Periodic deliveries: features delivered on a fixed schedule, similar to continuous delivery in DevOps.

subsections.title: Development Approaches
points: Predictive (waterfall): useful when work is easily defined, scope is collected upfront, investment is large or risk is high; relies on frequent reviews and change control.
Adaptive (agile): uses iterative and incremental approaches.
Iterative: feedback is gathered throughout but the final product is still delivered in one go.
Incremental: usable features are delivered progressively along the timeline while also taking feedback.
Hybrid: combines predictive and adaptive elements; likely the most common real-world approach.

subsections.title: Selecting a Development Approach
points: Product factors: degree of innovation needed, certainty of requirements, scope stability, ease of change, delivery increment options, risk and regulatory requirements.
Project factors: availability of a product owner, schedule constraints, and funding availability.
Organizational factors: flat vs. bureaucratic structure, culture, capability, and team size and location.

subsections.title: Life Cycle Phases
points: Common phases: feasibility, design, build, test, deploy/release, and close.
Waterfall executes each phase once across the entire project.
Adaptive (agile) iterates through all phases for each increment delivered.
Kanban uses a flow-based, pull approach with no fixed phases — work is pulled only when capacity is available.

subsections.title: Aligning Cadence, Approach, and Life Cycle
points: Cadence covers the rhythm and meetings, supporting single or multiple deliveries.
Development approach choices — predictive, hybrid, or adaptive (incremental/iterative) — must match project needs.
The life cycle (design, build, test, deploy, close) is applied differently depending on the chosen approach.

sections.title: Planning Domain
sections.intro: The Planning performance domain covers how projects progress in an organized, deliberate manner by using evolving information to deliver outputs and adapt plans to emerging needs.

subsections.title: Outcomes and Key Terms
points: Project progresses in an organized, deliberate manner using evolving information to produce deliverables.
Plans are adjusted as needed based on emerging needs or conditions.
Accuracy: how correct an estimate is — did it hit the target?
Precision: how repeatable an estimate is — can it do it over and over?
Schedule crashing: throwing money and resources at a schedule to bring the end date closer.
Schedule fast tracking: doing activities in parallel to compress the schedule.

subsections.title: Planning Variables
points: Development style: predictive (lots of upfront planning) vs. adaptive/rolling-wave (plan each increment in detail only as it approaches).
Project deliverables: construction may need more upfront planning; software may be more flexible.
Organizational requirements: governance policies, processes, and culture may dictate certain ways of working.
Market conditions: speed-to-market pressures may influence how quickly plans must be set.
Legal or regulatory restrictions must be factored into planning.

subsections.title: Delivery Planning Foundations
points: Planning begins with understanding the business case and feasibility study.
Stakeholder requirements define what is actually needed.
Product scope = all features in the product; project scope = the work performed to deliver those features.
Break both scopes down into a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) so individuals or teams can act on them.

subsections.title: Estimating
points: Estimates start broad (wide range) and become more refined as the project delivery date approaches.
Adjust estimates for uncertainty by building in reserves (extra funds/time for things going off track).
Use simulations such as mock-ups, storyboards, or prototypes to manage uncertainty.
Confidence in estimates increases with experience; deterministic = single point, probabilistic = range with probabilities.
Absolute estimates give a number; relative estimates compare to other work (e.g., story points in agile).
Flow-based estimates use cycle time (time to complete one item) and throughput (items completed per period).

subsections.title: Scheduling
points: Decompose project scope into specific activities via WBS, then sequence them (e.g., Gantt chart).
Estimate effort, duration, and required people/resources; allocate them, then adjust until the schedule is agreed.
Leads: pulling an activity forward (e.g., start editing photos partway through a shoot).
Lags: delaying an activity (e.g., waiting for concrete to set before framing).
Dependency types: mandatory (cannot change), discretionary (can change), external (outside project), internal (within project).
Rolling-wave planning: far-out items kept at high level; near-term items planned in detail.

subsections.title: Budgeting
points: Estimates applied to project work create the cost baseline.
Add a contingency reserve (for known-unknown risks) to reach the project budget.
Add a management reserve (for unexpected activities) on top to reach the total project cost.

subsections.title: Team Composition and Structure
points: Choose between internal and external team members based on cost, expertise, and location.
Consider whether proximity is required or whether a distributed global team is acceptable.

subsections.title: Communication Planning
points: Good communication planning helps engage stakeholders effectively.
Key questions: who needs information, what do they need, why share it, how to deliver it, when and how often, and where does the information come from.

subsections.title: Physical Resources Planning
points: Covers all non-human resources: estimating quantities, managing supply chain and logistics.
Consider lead times for delivery, movement, transport, and storage of materials.
Order materials strategically so they are available when needed without causing delays.

subsections.title: Procurement Planning
points: Once scope is known, perform a make-or-buy analysis.
Compare upfront cost of buying externally vs. making in-house AND the ongoing costs — ongoing costs are often overlooked.

subsections.title: Planning for Change
points: Prepare a process for adapting the plan throughout the project lifecycle.
Change mechanisms include: formal change control process, re-prioritizing the product backlog with the product owner, and re-baselining artifacts.
Change may arise from environmental shifts, customer requests, or a deeper understanding of scope.

subsections.title: Metrics and Alignment
points: Metrics define how project work is measured; a dedicated Measurement domain covers this in depth.
All planning artifacts must remain integrated: requirements align with scope; scope aligns with schedule and cost plans.
Quality, testing, logistics, and resource plans must all align with one another.
The overall project management plan integrates every sub-plan together.

sections.title: Project Work Domain
sections.intro: The Project Work domain covers the ongoing activities needed to keep project execution efficient, including managing processes, communications, physical resources, procurements, and continuous learning.

subsections.title: Outcomes and Key Terms
points: Desired outcomes: efficient project performance, appropriate processes, good stakeholder communication, efficient resource and procurement management, and improved team capability through continuous learning.
Bid documents are proposals from sellers; bidder conferences gather ideas from multiple bidders.
Explicit knowledge can be codified into a process; tacit knowledge is personal (beliefs, experience).

subsections.title: Project Processes
points: Periodically review and tailor the project process to fit the current environment.
Use lean methods, retrospectives, and lessons learned to identify what is working and what needs improvement.
Apply cost-benefit analysis and value stream mapping to evaluate process changes.
Balancing competing constraints (scope, quality, cost, time) is an ongoing activity.
In agile projects scope is variable; in predictive/waterfall projects scope is usually fixed and other constraints are more variable.
Trade-off sliders visually show which constraints are fixed and which are flexible.

subsections.title: Maintaining Project Team Focus
points: Maintaining team focus is the project manager's responsibility.
Includes short- and long-term projections of progress toward goals.
Balance workload across the team to prevent overload or underutilisation.
Assess whether team members are satisfied with their work.

subsections.title: Communications and Engagement
points: Communications may be formal, informal, verbal, or written.
Information is collected through meetings and conversations or pulled from repositories.
Communications are distributed according to the communications plan.
Abundant ad hoc requests signal insufficient communication; respond by communicating more or in different ways.

subsections.title: Managing Physical Resources
points: Resources include materials and supplies from third parties.
Resource process steps: planning, ordering, transporting, storing, tracking, and controlling.
Large resource volumes require a formal logistics system documented in company policies.
Apply lean principles to eliminate waste: defects/rework, overproduction, waiting, unnecessary transport, excess inventory, excessive motion, and excessive processing.

subsections.title: Working with Procurements
points: Procurements involve contracts for materials, equipment, labor, or services.
Project managers work with contracting officers and technical experts; they typically do not write contracts themselves.
PM may help develop a request for proposal, statement of work, and terms and conditions.
Bidding documents: RFI (gather market info), RFP (complex scope needs proposals), RFQ (price is the main factor).
Vendor source selection is based on price, delivery terms, and vendor experience.
Once selected, update the project plan with vendor details; the vendor becomes a project stakeholder.

subsections.title: Monitoring New Work and Changes
points: Scope may evolve throughout the project life.
In adaptive projects, the PM works with the product owner to prioritise the product backlog; low-priority items may be deferred.
In predictive projects, a change request is raised, reviewed by the change control board, and assessed for impact on cost, quality, scope, and schedule.
Approved changes are added to project documents and communicated to all stakeholders.

subsections.title: Learning Throughout the Project
points: Maintain a knowledge management process to capture lessons learned via retrospectives or reviews.
Explicit knowledge (process-based, teachable) and tacit knowledge (beliefs and experience) both need to be captured.
Captured knowledge is stored for other projects to use in the future.

sections.title: Delivery Domain
sections.intro: The Delivery performance domain focuses on how projects contribute to business objectives by managing requirements, scope, quality, and deliverables to realize intended benefits.

subsections.title: Delivery Outcomes and Key Terms
points: Projects must contribute to business objectives and overall strategy.
Intended outcomes and benefits should be realized within the expected timeframe.
Stakeholders must accept and be satisfied with the deliverables.
Key terms: requirement, work breakdown structure (WBS), definition of done, quality, cost of quality.
A deliverable is an increment of value — a feature or product.

subsections.title: Delivering Value
points: Adaptive approaches (incremental/iterative) deliver value continuously along the journey.
Predictive approaches deliver value all at once at the end.
Value persists long after the project finishes.
A business case defines and monitors value from the very beginning.
Baseline documents within the project track value delivery across different deliverables.

subsections.title: Requirements Management
points: A requirement is a conditional capability that must be met to satisfy the customer need.
Requirements elicitation draws out requirements from customers and stakeholders.
Good requirements are clear, concise, verifiable, consistent, complete, and traceable.
Evolving requirements can be discovered using prototypes, storyboards, and mock-ups.
Ineffective requirements lead to rework, scope creep, dissatisfaction, budget overruns, schedule delays, and project failure.
Manage requirements using a backlog or traceability matrix; one person is ultimately accountable for deliverables.

subsections.title: Scope Decomposition
points: Scope decomposition uses a WBS: themes break into epics, then into user stories people can deliver.
Alternatively, a product roadmap breaks themes into features, then into work packages.
Regardless of naming, the concept is the same: progressively decompose scope into workable items.

subsections.title: Defining Completion
points: Completion criteria include acceptance criteria, technical performance measures, definition of ready, and definition of done.
Done drift occurs when targets shift due to uncertainty, changing markets, or evolving project conditions.
Moving targets of completion are common in projects operating in uncertain environments.

subsections.title: Quality and Cost of Quality
points: Quality requirements are reflected in completion criteria, definition of done, statement of work, and requirements documentation.
Those who receive the benefit ultimately bear the cost of bad quality.
Prevention costs: effort spent stopping defects from occurring in the first place.
Appraisal costs: checking work during development to find bad quality early.
Internal failure costs: defects found before reaching the customer but still requiring rework.
External failure costs: the most expensive — defects discovered by the customer after delivery.

subsections.title: Sub-optimal Outcomes
points: Projects may not always meet their intended outcomes due to complexity and uncertainty.
Risks include uncertain environments, changing markets, or competitors reaching the market first.
Effective project management reduces but cannot eliminate risk — risk must always be managed.

sections.title: Measurement Domain
sections.intro: The Measurement performance domain focuses on establishing reliable metrics, baselines, and dashboards to understand project status, support timely decisions, and generate business value.

subsections.title: Outcomes and Key Terms
points: Desired outcomes: reliable understanding of project status, actionable data, timely actions, achieving targets, and generating business value.
Key terms include metrics, baselines (schedule, scope, cost), and dashboards.
Measurement is used to evaluate performance vs. plan, track resources, demonstrate accountability, and inform trade-off conversations.

subsections.title: Establishing Effective Measures
points: Use KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) or OKRs (Objectives and Key Results) to guide measurement.
Leading indicators: project size, backlog items remaining, lack of clear processes.
Lagging indicators: deliverables completed, schedule/cost variance already incurred, resources consumed — used to assess if the project is on track.
Effective metrics are SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Timely.

subsections.title: What to Measure: Deliverable and Delivery Metrics
points: Deliverable metrics: errors/defects, performance, efficiency, reliability, and technical performance measures.
Delivery metrics: work in progress, lead time, cycle time, queue size, and batch size (e.g., story points per sprint).

subsections.title: Project Efficiency and Baseline Performance
points: Compare planned vs. actual start/finish dates, effort, and duration.
Track schedule variance (SV) and Schedule Performance Index (SPI).
Track feature completion rates, actual vs. planned cost, cost variance (CV), and Cost Performance Index (CPI).
Measure planned vs. actual resource use and resource cost.

subsections.title: Business Value and Stakeholder Metrics
points: Business value metrics: cost-benefit ratio, planned vs. actual benefits delivery, ROI, and NPV.
Stakeholder metrics: Net Promoter Score (NPS) for engagement, mood charts for team morale, and team turnover.
Forecast metrics: Estimate to Complete (ETC), Estimate at Completion (EAC), Variance at Completion (VAC), and To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI).
Use regression analysis for trend forecasting and throughput analysis to track flow speed.

subsections.title: Presenting Information
points: Use dashboards and information radiators (e.g., visible backlogs, burndown charts, risk displays).
Visual controls include task boards, burn charts, and other project charts.

subsections.title: Measurement Pitfalls
points: Hawthorne Effect: measuring something changes people's behavior, so choose measures carefully.
Vanity metrics: figures that look good but do not drive meaningful outcomes.
Demoralization: unachievable targets demotivate the team.
Misusing metrics: using data for harmful rather than constructive purposes.
Confirmation bias: seeking data that confirms a pre-existing belief rather than the truth.
Correlation vs. causation: concurrent events do not imply a causal relationship.

subsections.title: Troubleshooting and Intent of Measurement
points: Agree in advance on plans for responding when measurements fall outside threshold ranges.
The intent of displaying data is to learn and improve.
Report information that facilitates real decisions, helps avoid future issues, or prevents performance breakdown.

sections.title: Uncertainty and Risk Domain
sections.intro: This domain covers how to identify and respond to uncertainty, ambiguity, complexity, volatility, and risk in order to minimize negative impacts and realize opportunities on the project.

subsections.title: Domain Outcomes and Key Terms
points: Desired outcomes: awareness of the political, environmental, social, technological, and legal environment (PESTLE).
Proactively explore uncertainty and understand the interdependence of multiple project variables (complexity).
Anticipate threats and opportunities and understand their consequences on delivery.
Minimize impact of unforeseen events; realize opportunities to improve performance.
Use cost and schedule reserves to meet objectives when uncertain events occur.
Key terms: uncertainty, ambiguity, complexity, volatility, risk; frameworks PESTLE and VUCA.

subsections.title: General Uncertainty
points: Gather information to reduce unknowns before committing.
Prepare for multiple outcomes so the team is not caught off guard.
Use set-based design or prototyping—build models before spending real money.
Build resilience into the process and be ready to respond or change quickly.

subsections.title: Ambiguity
points: Conceptual ambiguity: a general lack of understanding of an item.
Situational ambiguity: more than one outcome is possible and it is unclear which path to take.
Use progressive elaboration—deliver smaller features more often to work through different paths.
Run experiments with smaller items before full delivery to save cost.
Use prototyping to clarify requirements before committing to a solution.

subsections.title: Complexity
points: Complexity: a characteristic that makes something difficult to manage due to many interconnected influences.
Decouple interconnected parts to reduce the number of variables in play.
Use Monte Carlo simulation (thousands of runs with different variables) to see the range of possible outcomes.
Reframe complexity by bringing in diverse perspectives.
Balance lagging and leading data; use iterative or incremental delivery to uncover complexity gradually.
Engage stakeholders more and error-proof (fail-safe) the deliverable where possible.

subsections.title: Volatility
points: Volatility: something subject to rapid or unpredictable change.
Perform alternatives analysis—examine different options and weigh pros and cons.
Use contingency reserves and management reserves to absorb the impact if volatile events occur.

subsections.title: Risk — Threats
points: Capture each risk with its probability of occurring and its impact if it does occur.
Avoid: eliminate the threat entirely.
Escalate: pass the threat to a manager or sponsor if it is outside the team's authority.
Transfer: shift the threat to another department or organization (e.g., insurance).
Mitigate: apply controls to reduce probability or impact.
Accept: acknowledge the risk if it is within the organization's risk tolerance.

subsections.title: Risk — Opportunities
points: Exploit: take action to ensure the opportunity definitely occurs.
Escalate: refer the opportunity to management if approval is needed.
Share: partner with another department or organization to capture the opportunity.
Enhance: increase the probability or impact of the opportunity.
Accept: be ready to take advantage of the opportunity if it arises without actively pursuing it.
Maintain management and contingency reserves and conduct regular risk reviews.

sections.title: Tailoring
sections.intro: Tailoring is the deliberate adaptation of project management approach, governance, or processes to suit the specific environment, size, duration, complexity, and maturity of the organization and project.

subsections.title: Why Tailor?
points: Tailoring should reflect the size, duration, and complexity of the project.
Adapted to the industry and the project management maturity of the organization.
Less experienced teams may use an out-of-the-box method; more experienced teams may tailor to their individual needs.
Goal is to suit the situation and achieve successful delivery.

subsections.title: What to Tailor
points: Life cycle and development approach: combine predictive (waterfall), hybrid, iterative, incremental, and adaptive/agile approaches.
Processes: decide which portions to add, modify, remove, blend, or align (e.g., consistent risk definitions).
Engagement: choose who to involve, determine appropriate empowerment vs. supervision based on experience.
Integration: build a diverse project team including external members.
Tools: select software or equipment considering cost, organizational preferences, and existing assets.
Methods and artifacts: tailor documents and methods to be appropriate for the project and organization.

subsections.title: The Tailoring process
points: Step 1 — Select the initial development approach, applying knowledge of the product, delivery cadence, and meeting rhythm; use a suitability filter (predictive for high risk/long timeframe; adaptive for changing requirements/early value delivery).
Step 2 — Tailor for the organization: consider existing methodology, established governance, and any contract terms.

Step 3 — Tailor for the project: consider compliance/criticality, industry/technology, timeframe, requirement stability, security, team size/geography, experience, customer access, and organizational culture.
Step 4 — Implement ongoing improvements: assess whether the approach is working and continuously improve it.

subsections.title: Tailoring the Performance Domains
points: Stakeholders: consider collaborative environment, internal vs. external stakeholders, available communication technology, language/jargon diversity, number of stakeholders, and existing relationships.
Team: consider co-located vs. dispersed, diverse cultures/viewpoints, internal vs. contractor composition, existing team culture, tools and training needs, and special requirements.
Development approach and life cycle: choose approach based on speed, quality, and scope needs; check for existing formal/informal audit or governance policies.
Planning: assess environmental factors, resource productivity, cost-estimating policies, adaptive process existence, procurement complexity, and contracting laws or regulations.
Project work and delivery: address knowledge management, lessons learned repositories, requirements management systems, change/quality control policies, industry standards, and unstable requirements.

Uncertainty and measurement: evaluate risk appetite, threat/opportunity identification processes, complexity impact, project strategic importance, how value (financial and non-financial) is measured, and status reporting requirements.

sections.title: Models
sections.intro: Models help explain how things work in the real world; PMBOK 7 covers situational leadership, communication, motivation, change, complexity, team development, conflict, negotiation, planning, process groups, and stakeholder salience models.

subsections.title: Situational leadership and coaching models
points: Ken Blanchard's Situational Leadership II: measures a person's competence and commitment to adapt leadership style accordingly.
OSCAR coaching model: focuses on Outcome, current Situation, Choices/Consequences, Actions, and Review.

subsections.title: Communication models
points: Cross-cultural communication: messages are shaped by sender's and receiver's knowledge, experience, language, and communication style — must be adjusted for differences.
Alistair Cockburn's communication channel effectiveness: measured by richness (multiple cues, rapid feedback, natural language) and effectiveness over time; face-to-face is highest richness.

Donald Norman's Gulf of Execution and Evaluation: Gulf of Execution asks whether a system does what the user expected; Gulf of Evaluation asks whether it supports users in discovering how to interact with it.

subsections.title: Motivation models
points: Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory: hygiene factors (salary, fair policies, physical environment) prevent dissatisfaction; motivational factors (achievement, growth) drive true motivation.
Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic motivation: intrinsic = autonomy, mastery, purpose; extrinsic = money, bonuses, status.
McClelland's Theory of Needs: people are driven by achievement, power, or affiliation.
McGregor's Theory X, Y, Z: X = people motivated only by income, need micromanagement; Y = intrinsically motivated, managed as a coach; Z = motivated by a higher calling, job-for-life orientation.

subsections.title: Change models
points: PMI Change Management model: formulate, plan, implement, sustain the change, then manage the transition to the new state.
ADKAR model: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, and Reinforcement of change.
Kotter's 8 Steps: create urgency, form a coalition, create a vision, communicate the vision, remove obstacles, create short-term wins, build on change, anchor changes in culture.
Virginia Satir Change Model: late status quo → foreign element → chaos → transforming idea → practice and integration → new status quo.
William Bridges Transition Model: ending (losing and letting go) → neutral zone → new beginning.

subsections.title: Complexity models
points: Cynefin Framework (Dave Snowden): obvious = use best practices; complicated = assess facts and use good practices; complex/unknown unknowns = probe and iterate; chaotic = stabilize first; disordered = break into smaller parts.
Stacey Matrix (Ralph Stacy): plots uncertainty of deliverable and technology against simplicity, complexity, or chaos to guide approach.

subsections.title: Team development models
points: Tuckman's Ladder: Forming → Storming (conflict over roles) → Norming (establishing rhythms) → Performing → Adjourning.
Drexler-Sibbet Team Performance Model: Orientation (why) → Trust building (who does what) → Goal clarification (what) → Commitment (how) → Implementation (plan) → High performance → Renewal.

subsections.title: Conflict, negotiation, planning, process groups, and stakeholder models
points: Conflict model: approaches include confront/problem-solve (collaborate), compromise, smooth/accommodate, force, or withdraw/avoid.
Negotiation approaches: win-win (requires character, maturity, and trust), win-lose, lose-win, or lose-lose (compromise is typically lose-lose since both parties give something up).
Barry Boehm's Planning Sweet Spot: balance between upfront planning to reduce risk and time-to-market speed — optimum lies somewhere in the middle.
PMI Process Groups model: Initiating → Planning → Executing → Monitoring and Controlling → Closing.
Salience Model: evaluates stakeholders by power, legitimacy, and urgency to prioritize engagement.

sections.title: Methods
sections.intro: A method is a means for achieving an outcome, result, or deliverable; PMBOK 7 groups commonly used methods into data gathering and analysis, estimating, meetings and events, and other methods.

subsections.title: Data Gathering and Analysis
points: Alternatives analysis, assumption and constraint analysis, benchmarking across teams or organizations
Business justification analysis: payback period, internal rate of return, ROI, NPV, cost-benefit analysis
Check sheets, cost of quality (prevention, appraisal, internal/external failure), decision tree analysis
Earned value analysis, expected monetary value, forecasting, influence diagrams, life cycle assessment
Make-or-buy analysis, probability and impact matrix, process analysis (flowcharts), regression analysis

Reserve analysis, root cause analysis (Ishikawa/5-Whys), sensitivity analysis (tornado chart), simulations (Monte Carlo), stakeholder analysis, SWOT, trend analysis, value stream mapping, variance analysis, what-if scenario analysis

subsections.title: Estimating Methods
points: Affinity grouping and analogous estimating (based on similar past work)
Function point metrics (business functionality in information systems)
Multi-point estimating: (optimistic + most likely + pessimistic) / 3
Parametric estimating (uses a unit parameter, e.g. $80/hour)
Relative estimating (agile; estimates relative to each other) and single-point estimating
Story point estimating using Fibonacci sequence (1,2,3,5,8,13,21) and wideband Delphi (iterative rounds converging on accuracy)

subsections.title: Meetings and Events
points: Agile/adaptive ceremonies: iteration planning, backlog refinement, daily stand-ups, iteration review, retrospectives
Kick-off meeting and change control board
Bid conferences and lessons learned sessions
Planning meetings, project closeout meetings, project reviews
Release planning, risk review meetings, status meetings, steering committee meetings

subsections.title: Other Methods
points: Impact mapping and modeling
Net Promoter Score: 'on a scale of 1–10 how likely to recommend?' used as a customer metric
Prioritization schemas such as MoSCoW (Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have)
Time boxing an item

sections.title: Artifacts
sections.intro: Artifacts are the templates, documents, and deliverables commonly produced across projects, grouped into eight categories: strategic artifacts, logs and registers, plans, hierarchy charts, baselines, visual data and reports, agreements and contracts, and other artifacts.

subsections.title: Strategic Artifacts
points: Business case
Business model canvas
Project brief and project charter
Project vision statement
Product roadmap

subsections.title: Logs and Registers
points: Assumption log
Backlog and risk-adjusted backlog (risk items folded into the backlog)
Change log and issue log
Lessons learned register
Risk register
Stakeholder register

subsections.title: Plans
points: Change control plan, communications management plan, cost management plan
Iteration plan (agile), procurement management plan
Project management plan (ties all subsidiary plans together)
Quality management plan, release plan, requirements management plan
Resource management plan, risk management plan, scope management plan
Schedule management plan, stakeholder engagement plan, test plan

subsections.title: Hierarchy Charts
points: Organizational breakdown structure (OBS)
Product breakdown structure (PBS)
Resource breakdown structure (RBS)
Risk breakdown structure
Work breakdown structure (WBS)

subsections.title: Baselines
points: Budget baseline (original cost estimate)
Milestone schedule baseline
Performance measurement baseline (scope, schedule, cost)
Project schedule baseline
Scope baseline: scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary

subsections.title: Visual Data and Reports
points: Affinity diagrams, burn-up/burn-down charts, cause-and-effect diagrams, cumulative flow diagrams, cycle-time charts
Dashboards, flow charts, Gantt charts, histograms, information radiators, lead-time charts
Prioritization matrix, schedule network diagram, requirements traceability matrix, resource assignment matrix
Scatter diagram, S-curve, stakeholder engagement assessment matrix, story map
Throughput chart, use cases, value stream maps, velocity charts
Quality report, risk report, status report

subsections.title: Agreements and Contracts
points: Fixed-price contracts
Cost-reimbursable contracts: cost plus award fee, cost plus fixed fee, cost plus incentive fee
Time and materials contracts
Indefinite delivery / indefinite quantity (IDIQ) contracts
Other agreements: memorandum of understanding (MOU), memorandum of agreement (MOA), service level agreement (SLA), basic ordering agreement (BOA)

subsections.title: Other Artifacts
points: Activity list
Bid documents: request for information (RFI), request for quote (RFQ), request for proposal (RFP)
Metrics
Project calendar and project team charter
Requirements documentation
User stories
